coredata.dc:title: Reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching of repeated-response cellulose-based hydrogels for information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yu
Wang, Zhiqi
Quan, Lingqi
Wu, Yiqiang
Hu, Dongying
Cheng, Jun
Zheng, Yanjie
Cheng, Fangchao
coredata.dc:description: The rapid development of anti-counterfeiting technology has brought new challenges to the repeatability and stability of reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching hydrogels. To address this issue, a series of chemical cross-linked cellulose-based intelligent responsive hydrogels were synthesized by free-radical graft copolymerization in a hydrothermal process. This strategy allows for the creation of a chemical cross-linked three-dimensional structure that anchors photochromic ammonium molybdate and fluorescent carbon dots together, resulting in enhanced stability and mechanical properties. Especially, the tensile and compressive strength of hydrogel reached a maximum value of 280 kPa and 560 kPa, respectively, which far exceeds that of some reported hydrogels. The resultant hydrogels exhibited desired reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching, reversible printing and erasing of patterns, and information encryption/decryption. Notably, the change of photochromism from yellow to green can be realized, and the self-fading process can be shortened to 25 min at 60 °C instead of 6 h at room temperature. More importantly, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the hydrogel occurs gradually after 2 min of continuous irradiation, and it can be recovered by selective treatment with ethanol. Overall, this study provides a simple strategy for the preparation of environmentally friendly reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching cellulose-based hydrogels for information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Fluorescence
Photochromic
Anti-counterfeiting
Information encryption/decryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Fabrication of fluorescent/photochromic reversibly switchable CCA hydrogels, b) photochromic mechanism and c) molecular changes in photo fluorescence in CCA hydrogels, d) achieving reversible pattern printing and erasing of CCA-0.2 hydrogel.

a) TEM image of CDS with particle size distribution and lattice, b) physical images of CCA-0.2 before and after UV irradiation and the SEM image, c) FT-IR spectra of CCA-0.2, CDs, and CMC, d) C1s spectra of CCA-0.2, e) O1s spectra of CCA-0.2, f) N1s spectra of CCA-0.2, g) Intermolecular interaction mechanism of CCA, h) compressive strength and i) tensile strength of CCA hydrogels with different amounts of CDs.

a) Emission spectra of CCA hydrogels at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, b) schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching, c) excitation and emission spectra of CCA-0.2, insetting an optical photograph, d) molecular change mechanism of fluorescence, e) emission spectra of CCA-0.2 at 365 nm after UV irradiation at different times, f) fluorescence spectra of hydrogel irradiated by ultraviolet light at different times.

a) The reflectance of CCA-0.2 after UV light irradiation at different durations, b) photochromism behavior of CCA-0.2 with the increase of UV irradiation time and the fluorescence behavior after UV irradiation for different durations, c) the reflectance of photochromic CCA-0.2 after being placed at 25 °C for different times, d) fading process of CCA-0.2 after photochromism at 25 °C, e) fading process of CCA-0.2 after photochromism at 60 °C, f) the reflectance of CCA-0.2 hydrogel during photochromic and discoloration, g) cycling stability of CCA-0.2 at 540 nm.

a) Schematic diagram of fluorescence transformation with the addition of ethanol, b) optical images of CCA-0.2 at 25 °C and under UV light, as well as mechanism of fluorescence quenching and recovery after ethanol immersion, c) FT-IR spectra of CCA-0.2 before and after photochromic, d) Mo 3d spectra of CCA-0.2, e) Mo 3d spectra of CCA-0.2 after UV irradiation, f) reversible pattern printing and erasing of CCA-0.2, g) information encryption/decryption process of CCA-0.2 hydrogel.
Conditions adopted for hydrogel optimization experiment.

head.ce:title: Reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching of repeated-response cellulose-based hydrogels for information encryption

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Featured Metal Materials and Life-cycle Safety for Composite Structures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid development of anti-counterfeiting technology has brought new challenges to the repeatability and stability of reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching hydrogels. To address this issue, a series of chemical cross-linked cellulose-based intelligent responsive hydrogels were synthesized by free-radical graft copolymerization in a hydrothermal process. This strategy allows for the creation of a chemical cross-linked three-dimensional structure that anchors photochromic ammonium molybdate and fluorescent carbon dots together, resulting in enhanced stability and mechanical properties. Especially, the tensile and compressive strength of hydrogel reached a maximum value of 280 kPa and 560 kPa, respectively, which far exceeds that of some reported hydrogels. The resultant hydrogels exhibited desired reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching, reversible printing and erasing of patterns, and information encryption/decryption. Notably, the change of photochromism from yellow to green can be realized, and the self-fading process can be shortened to 25 min at 60 °C instead of 6 h at room temperature. More importantly, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the hydrogel occurs gradually after 2 min of continuous irradiation, and it can be recovered by selective treatment with ethanol. Overall, this study provides a simple strategy for the preparation of environmentally friendly reversible fluorescence/photochromic switching cellulose-based hydrogels for information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Counterfeiting is a global problem that cannot be ignored. In recent decades, new anti-counterfeiting materials such as holograms, watermarks, anti-counterfeiting codes, and encryption/decryption technologies have led to unprecedented developments in anti-counterfeiting [1]. Among the currently available anti-counterfeiting materials, fluorescent materials such as organic fluorescent dyes [2,3], quantum dots [4], carbon dots [5], and nanoclusters [6,7] have been used to construct fluorescent sensors, which are widely used in the anti-counterfeiting industry. They can be easily dispersed in matrices and exhibit sufficient stability to enable fluorescent sensors to achieve excellent accuracy and reproducibility. Furthermore, hydrogels have excellent ductility and flexibility, which are widely used in the fields of ink-free printing, erasable displays and wearable flexible devices [8,9]. Recent research has demonstrated the potential of incorporating compounds with fluorescent sensing properties (carbon dots, rare earth oxides, polymer luminescent materials) into hydrogels to realize erasable displays [10–12], which can be used for pattern printing and information encryption in anti-counterfeiting field.

However, the rapid development of the anti-counterfeiting field has placed new demands on fluorescence technology. In the field of fluorescence technology, although the fluorescent hydrogels have gained considerable interest due to their smart responsiveness, these materials still exhibit certain limitations that restrict their use in practical applications, particularly in the development of flexible wearable devices. For instance, the hydrogels typically exhibit a transient response [13], some of the fluorescent hydrogels display inadequate mechanical properties [11], and a complex preparation process is usually required [14]. At the same time, the majority of reported fluorescent hydrogels possess a single light-emitting center, which presents a challenge in utilizing their fluorescent characteristics in information security and encryption applications [15]. Nowadays, photochromic materials are gradually attracting attention due to their ability to undergo reversible color changes in response to specific light conditions, and these materials exhibit excellent sustained light response and reversibility. In addition, the response time and fading time of photochromic materials are longer than those of fluorescent materials. Therefore, we are seeking a strategy to incorporate photochromic materials into fluorescent sensing hydrogels, which is expected to combine the advantages of both to simultaneously achieve excellent mechanical properties, fast response and long-lasting photochromism, and to be able to reversibly switch between fluorescent and photochromic for better anti-counterfeiting applications.

To verify the above hypothesis, the clever combination of carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent elements and ammonium molybdate as photochromic component was introduced into carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) based matrix to form CMC/CDs/ammonium molybdate hydrogels (CCA). CMC is biodegradable, non-toxic, non-polluting and inexpensive compared to other polymeric materials. It can easily undergo physical and chemical reactions with other inorganic complexes or polymers due to the large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups present in the chain segments of the molecule [16,17]. The three-dimensional cross-linked network structure of CCA hydrogels provides the anchoring contribution of photochromic and fluorescent chromic components by free-radical graft copolymerization using N, N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) as a cross-linking agent in a hydrothermal process.

As expected, the cellulose-derived hydrogels exhibit excellent photochromic and fluorescent properties under UV irradiation due to the presence of CDs and Mo, and long-lasting fluorescence response and repeatable erasable photochromism can be achieved. In addition, the combination of carbon dots with ammonium molybdate resulted in the presence of a large number of reactive functional groups on the surface, providing excellent photostability and photochromic properties through potential light-induced proton and electron transfer processes. Meanwhile, the addition of carbon dots can stabilize the water content of the hydrogel by facilitating the retention of water within the matrix. Interestingly, a fluorescence quenching occurred during the photochromic process, and the fluorescence was restored after the addition of ethanol to the hydrogel. This process can be repeated many times for multiple reversible storage/erasure of optical information and encryption/decryption of information in pattern security. Therefore, this study provides a valid strategy for the fabrication of environmentally friendly photochromic/fluorescent bifunctional materials, which are expected to be applied in the fields of pattern printing, information storage, and anti-counterfeiting.

Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, USR), with a substitution degree of 0.7–0.9 and molecular weight of 250,000 g·mol−1, acrylic acid (AA, AR), ammonium molybdate (Mo, AR), N, N′- methylenebisacrylamide (MBA, AR), and ammonium persulfate (APS, AR) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (CA, AR), anhydrous ethylene diamine (EDA, AR), and absolute ethyl alcohol (EtOH, AR) were provided by Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water.

In a typical process, 1.05 g of CA was firstly dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and then 375 μL of EDA was added to form a uniform solution. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, the above solution was transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and it was heated at 160 °C for 4 h and allowed to cool to room temperature before use. The sample was centrifuged at 8000 r min−1 for 10 min to obtain CDs [18].

1.5 g of CMC and 18 g of AA solution were mixed in 25 mL of deionized water to give a transparent solution (A). 1.8 g of ammonium molybdate and a certain mass of CDs were added into 4 mL of deionized water to obtain a transparent solution (B). 0.07 g of MBA and 0.7 g of APS was mixed in 1 mL of deionized water solution to obtain mixed solution (C). C was transferred into a homogeneous mixture of A and B and stirred for 1 min, followed by transferred to a PTFE-lined reactor and heated at different temperatures [19]. The reaction was freely cooled to 25 °C to obtain the CCA hydrogels. The hydrogels prepared with the different amounts of CDs added and different hydrothermal temperatures represent CCA-0.1, CCA-0.2, CCA-0.6, CCA-40, CCA-60, and CCA-80, respectively, as shown in Table 1 .

The prepared hydrogel and CDs aqueous solution were ultrasonic dispersed and dropped into the carbon coated grid. The morphology and distribution of CDs and hydrogels were observed using a FEI TECNAI G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM; FEI, USA) with an operating voltage of 300 kV.

The hydrogel was frozen in a cold trap at −55 °C, and then freeze-dried under vacuum at −64 °C for 24 h. The morphology of freeze-dried hydrogel samples after gold spraying was observed with Sigma300 scanning electron microscope (SEM; Carl Zeiss AG) at 5 kV voltage. The hydrogel was characterized by SU8220 field emission scanning electron microscope. Collect secondary electron images of dry samples under the condition of accelerating the working voltage from 5 kV to 15 kV [20].

The freeze-dried hydrogel was cut into tiny sheets, mixed with potassium bromide under infrared light, and grinded into sheets. FTIR spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corp, USA) in the wavelength range of 4000–650 cm−1.
The possible forms of chemical bonding in the hydrogels were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). using an ESCALAB 250XI + spectrometer.

The reflectance changes of hydrogel under different irradiation times and fading times were determined by using a CARY ECLIPSE fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). A mold was used to cover the hydrogel surface, and then UV light was applied at a distance of 3 cm to observe the printability of the pattern at room temperature under UV light [21]. The reversible recyclability of CCA-0.2 hydrogel was tested by observing the natural fading process under visible light irradiation for several times.

The tensile and compressive tests were performed on the ZQ990A mechanical testing machine at 25 ± 1 °C and 65 % ± 2 % RH (Dongguan Zhi Taking Precision Instrument Co., Ltd., Dongguan, China). The dimensions of the hydrogels for tensile and compression tests were 10 mm × 6 mm × 2 mm (length × width × height) and 15 mm × 10 mm (diameter × height), respectively. The equipment information for characterizations was provided in Table S1.
The tensile/compressive mechanical properties and the reversible repeatability of CCA hydrogel were tested repeatedly. The mean value was used and the standard deviation was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green preparation strategy for the fluorescent/photochromic hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the reversible fluorescent/photochromic switching with excellent pattern printing and erasing, a strategy based on green carboxymethyl cellulose and chemical cross-linking was carried out (Fig. 1 ). A photochromic/fluorescence switching hydrogel was prepared using CMC and AA as the matrix of the cross-linked network structure, MBA as the cross-linking agent, and CD and ammonium molybdate as the fluorescence and photochromic measuring agents, respectively (Fig. 1a). In terms of cross-linking agent and initiator of MBA and APS, CMC and AA were grafted and polymerized through radical polymerization and esterification reactions to form a cross-linked network structure of CMC-AA complexes. Further, ammonium molybdate was dissolved in solution to form Mo7O24 6−, which interacted with the carbonyl groups on the chain segments of the CMC-AA complexes. Fig. 1b-c shows the mechanism of photochromic and fluorescence changes of molecular interaction in CCA hydrogels, where the photochemical reaction occurs under UV light irradiation. Due to the charge transfer leading to the color change, some of the colorless Mo6+ in ammonium molybdate was reduced to blue Mo5+ [22,23]. Meanwhile, since the original color of the hydrogel is light yellow, the mixture of blue and yellow after photochromism leads to a dark green color of the hydrogel. After the completion of the photochromic process, oxygen in the air can capture an electron from Mo5+ to oxidize Mo5+ to Mo6+ [22,24]. Therefore, the color of the hydrogel after photochromism can be restored from dark green to the original light yellow under natural conditions, and this process is accelerated under heating conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the schematic of the process of reversible writing and erasing pattern of CCA hydrogel indicates that the hydrogel has excellent photochromic characteristics. Under UV irradiation, hydrogels with different templates were obtained with different patterns. In the air environment, the surface pattern of the photochromic hydrogel gradually returned to the color of the original hydrogel within 6 h. Therefore, the hydrogel can be erased and rewritten repeatedly and reproducibly, which indicates that this green strategy can realize the excellent reversible and repetitive patterning and information recording .

The physiochemically cross-linked network structure of the CMC-AA hydrogel plays an important role in the loading, fixation and reversible conversion of photochromic/fluorescent components (Fig. 2 ). Firstly, the HRTEM image shows that the CDs were nanoparticles with an average particle size of 2.61 nm, which were uniformly distributed in the aqueous solution, and there was no agglomeration phenomenon (Fig. 2a, and Fig. S1). The CDs lattice spacing of 0.22 nm was close to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite (inset of Fig. 2a). It indicates that the structure of the prepared CDs was similar to that of graphitic carbon, confirming the formation of carbon dots sp2 graphitic carbon during hydrothermal process, which is favorable for embedding. The physical image and SEM image of CCA-0.2 hydrogel after freeze-drying show that the prepared hydrogel was light yellow and exhibited excellent fluorescence properties under UV irradiation (Fig. 2b). It is shown that the CCA-0.2 hydrogel exhibited an obvious highly porous structure (Fig. S2). These cross-linked and interpenetrating porous structures provide physical support for the contribution of excellent mechanical strength.

Furthermore, the chemical structure of the CCA hydrogel was analyzed and verified using FTIR and XPS spectra. It can be seen from FTIR spectra of CCA-0.2, CDs, and CMC (Fig. 2c) that the characteristic peak at 3450 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1 was attributed to the OH and CH stretching vibration peaks [25,26]. The wider peaks of CMC at 3600–3200 cm−1 belonged to the OH group, which was because the hydroxyl groups on the molecular chain of cellulose were not completely replaced by carboxyl groups during the carboxymethyl reaction process [27]. Peaks at 1610 and 1420 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration peaks of COO, respectively [24,28], and the vibrational peak at 1121 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of COH [27]. For CDs, the characteristic peaks at 3600–3200 cm−1 belonged to the overlapping absorption peaks of OH and NH. The absorption peaks at 1710 and 1665 cm−1 were the CO stretching vibration peak and the NH stretching vibration peak in the COO group, respectively [29]. The carbonyl stretching vibration peak was usually used to determine the grafting situation. Compared with the characteristic peaks of CMC and CDs, the intensity of CCA-0.2 hydrogel at 3650–2750 cm−1 was lower than that of CMC, which was due to the intermolecular and intramolecular hydrogen bonds formed by the interaction between CMC and AA molecules as well as CDs [27]. The new characteristic peaks at 1701 and 1450 cm−1 attributed to the CO stretching vibration peak in the COO group and the COO symmetric stretching vibration peak in the COO group, respectively, which confirmed that the AA monomer was grafted onto the CMC molecular chain segment [30]. The peak at 920 cm−1 was attributed to the MoO characteristic peak, while the characteristic peaks at 875 and 798 cm−1 belonged to MoOMo [31,32]. At the same time, the peaks related to CDs partially disappeared with shifted positions or attenuated intensities, indicating that the amino or carboxylates on the carbon surface reacted with AA and CMC. The above reaction immobilized the CDs among the network of the hydrogel, which contributed to excellent mechanical properties and good fluorescence behavior.

Moreover, the chemical bonding and composition of the hydrogels were explored by XPS analysis. From the full-scan XPS spectra, it was able to observe distinct characteristic peaks at 233, 285, 399, and 532 eV, attributed to Mo3d, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S3). It indicated that the surface of CCA hydrogel was mainly composed of Mo, C, N, and O elements. The spectrum of C1s in Fig. 2d shows characteristic peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 286.1, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, C-OH, CN/CO, and CO [33,34]. The peaks appearing in the O1s spectra at 531.8 and 532.7 eV were characteristic of CO and OCO/C-OH (Fig. 2e) [35]. The peak presented at 399.6 eV was observed in the N1s spectra as a characteristic peak of CNC. The peaks at 400.3 and 401.8 eV corresponding to NH and N-(C)3 were characteristic peaks of pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen, respectively [34,36], which verified the chemical structure and constituent elements presented in the CCA-0.2 hydrogel (Fig. 2f). The intermolecular interaction mechanism of CCA obtained from the FTIR and XPS analysis is also shown in Fig. 2g, which is the key to contribute excellent mechanical properties.

For further studies, the compressive and tensile properties of the fluorescent/photochromic CCA hydrogels with different Mo: CDs ratios and different hydrothermal temperatures were investigated. The compressive strength of hydrogels increased significantly with increasing of hydrothermal temperatures and Mo: CDs ratio, reaching the highest compressive strength of 560 ± 52 kPa (Fig. 2h and Fig. S4), far exceeding some reported hydrogels: 41.47 kPa [37], 194 kPa [38], 316 kPa [39]. The tensile strength follows the same pattern. When the hydrothermal reaction temperature was 60 °C and Mo: CDs = 18:1, the tensile strength of CCA-0.1 hydrogel reached the maximum value of 280 ± 26 kPa (Fig. 2i and Fig. S5), far exceeding some reported hydrogels: 22.86 kPa [40], 62 kPa [41], 210 kPa [42]. Reversible information encryption materials must possess not only the capability for reversible stimulus response, but also high mechanical properties to better resist the damage caused by external tension or pressure on the material [8]. The above results indicated that the hydrothermal temperature and the Mo: CDs ratio played a key role in improving the mechanical strength of CCA hydrogels. The reason is that the thermal polymerization reaction is initiated by the formation of radical species from the homolysis of persulfate. By increasing the temperature, the amount of radical species increases and consequently the rate of polymerization will also increase. Therefore, CCA-0.2 hydrogel stands out in terms of both mechanical properties and desired fluorescent/photochromic properties.

Optical photos and fluorescence behaviors of CCA hydrogels prepared with different ratios and hydrothermal temperatures under UV light irradiation at 365 nm are represented in Fig. S6. The CCA-80 hydrogel prepared at a hydrothermal temperature of 80 °C was dark blue and had almost no fluorescence behavior under UV light. This indicates that the high hydrothermal temperature during the preparation process may lead to the conversion of Mo6+ to Mo5+ in the hydrogel, which changed the color of the hydrogel to dark blue and then masked the fluorescence phenomenon of the hydrogel. Therefore, by lowering the hydrothermal temperature, a series of hydrogels with a light-yellow color were successfully prepared. As expected, it could be seen that the color of the hydrogel at room temperature gradually became lighter with the decrease of the hydrothermal temperature and the increase of the ratio of Mo to CDs. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel also showed a certain degree of attenuation. When the hydrothermal temperature was 40 °C and Mo: CDs = 3:1, the CCA-0.6 hydrogel after the hydrothermal reaction appeared to be gelatinous, and its fluorescence phenomenon was also weak. It was due to the low hydrothermal temperature and the high content of CDs results that the cross-linking reaction of the hydrogel was not sufficient during the synthesis process. Through the above comprehensive analysis of the mechanical properties, appearance color, and fluorescence properties of the CCA hydrogel, the CCA-0.2 hydrogel prepared under the conditions of Mo: CDs = 9:1, hydrothermal temperature of 60 °C, and hydrothermal time of 2 h was confirmed for subsequent characterization and testing.

It can be seen from the emission spectra of different proportions of hydrogels at 365 nm (Fig. 3 a) that the fluorescence intensity of hydrogels gradually increases with the increase of CDs content. Yet, with the increase in hydrothermal temperature, the fluorescence intensities of the CCA hydrogel also showed a same trend of gradual increase. The above phenomenon was consistent with the optical picture of the CCA hydrogel in Fig. S6, thus further verifying the optical performance of the hydrogel. Fig. 3b is the schematic diagram of fluorescence quenching. The optical properties of the prepared hydrogel were further verified by the UV–visible spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. The UV–visible absorption spectrum shows that CCA-0.2 hydrogel has an obvious characteristic absorption peak at 360 nm (Fig. S7). The excitation-emission fluorescence spectrum of the hydrogel showed that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the hydrogel were 386 nm and 460 nm, respectively (Fig. 3c). As the optical photo described in the illustration shows, the pale-yellow hydrogel appeared bright green under the irradiation of the ultraviolet lamp. The comparison showed that the optimal excitation wavelength of the hydrogel was similar to the optimal ultraviolet–visible absorption wavelength, indicating that the unsaturated center CC molecule with low carbon point surface energy in the hydrogel undergoes n → π* and π → π* transitions [43,44]. When the excitation wavelength was 365 nm, the maximum intensity of its emission wavelength was concentrated at the wavelength of −460 nm, belonging to the surface structure of carbon dots and the sp2 cluster of the core. It is suggested that the radiative recombination of confined electrons and holes on the surface of CDs in hydrogels is the main source of the bright fluorescence of hydrogels [43,45]. Fig. 3d shows the molecular changes in the switching of fluorescence and quenching phenomena. The fluorescence intensity of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel decreased with the increase of UV irradiation time, indicating that the hydrogel undergoes fluorescence quenching behavior under continuous irradiation of UV light (Fig. 3e). The fluorescence spectra of hydrogels irradiated with UV light for different times were tested (Fig. 3f), and the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels gradually decreased with the increase of the UV light irradiation time, which further proved that the hydrogels would undergo fluorescence quenching.

The CCA hydrogel was irradiated with UV light using a xenon lamp light source to observe the photochromic behavior of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel as well as its effect on the fluorescence properties of the hydrogel during the photochromic process. Fig. 4 a-b shows that the CCA-0.2 hydrogel has fast photochromic responsiveness. The color of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel started to deepen after 1 min of UV irradiation and changed from light yellow to green after 2 min. And the color gradually deepened with increasing irradiation time, finally becoming dark green. At the same time, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel decreased with the increase of UV irradiation time. Moreover, the CCA-0.2 hydrogel showed a complete fluorescence quenching phenomenon under the UV light when it was irradiated for about 4 min. The process of color change of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel was determined using a spectrophotometer, and the refractive index of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel showed a gradual decrease with the increase of UV irradiation time. The relationship between the refractive index intensity at 540 nm and the UV irradiation time show that the controllable photochromic behavior of hydrogels can be achieved by adjusting the UV irradiation time (Fig. S8).

Furthermore, the color of the CCA-0.2 hydrogel after photochromism gradually recovered at room temperature until it completely faded. CCA-0.2 hydrogel was placed in a closed mold, the dark green hydrogel completely faded back to its original color after 6 h (Fig. 4c-d). The fading results of photochromic CCA-0.2 hydrogel show that the refractive index of the hydrogel gradually increased with the increase of storage time, and returned to the original refractive index of the hydrogel after 6 h, indicating that the hydrogel had excellent photobleaching performance (Table. S2). The nonlinear fitting between the refractive index intensity and the fading time at 540 nm wavelength (Fig. S9) shows that the fading time of the hydrogels could modulate the degree of fading of the photochromic hydrogels. Meanwhile, the temperature had a significant effect on the fading rate of hydrogel. Under the condition of 60 °C, the fading time of the hydrogel was shortened from 6 h to 25 min, greatly improving the fading efficiency of the hydrogel. In addition, the external environment also had a significant impact on the fading rate of photochromic hydrogels (Fig. S10). The results showed that hydrogels placed in bright or dark environments do not affect their fading behavior. However, when the hydrogel was placed in a vacuum atmosphere, the color of the hydrogel can change from dark green to light blue after 24 h. Compared with the fading behavior in air, the fading time of the hydrogel was significantly prolonged. This indicated that the photochromic performance is controlled by the oxidation process. In the air, oxygen would capture electrons from Mo5+ and slowly oxidize them to Mo6+, causing photochromic hydrogel to fade until it returned to its original color. The slow fading process under vacuum conditions further confirmed that the redox reaction had an impact on the fading behavior of hydrogels. Fig. 4f shows the refractive index of CCA-0.2 hydrogel after photochromic change for 6 min versus fading at room temperature for 6 h. It can be seen that the refractive index of the hydrogel after the photochromic change was lower than that before the change. To test the reversible repeatability of hydrogels, the refractive index change of the hydrogel at 540 nm was measured during the photochromic and fading processes, and the refractive index of the hydrogel showed stable photochromic performance with almost no change in at least five cycles (Fig. 4g). Therefore, the hydrogel exhibits excellent photochromic performance as well as reversible reproducibility, which can greatly promote the development and application of hydrogels in the field of functional photochromic devices and visual display devices.

Indeed, CCA-0.2 cross-linked by CMC, CDs, and ammonium molybdate underwent photochromic behavior and fluorescence quenching after 5 min of UV irradiation. Interestingly, a small amount of ethanol solution was immediately dropped into the hydrogel, and the fluorescence response of the mixture was restored (Fig. 5 a-b). The results showed that the interaction between CDs and molybdate as well as CMC took place to form a more stable complex before light irradiation. Under UV irradiation, various interactions between CDs and the surrounding polymetallic molybdates as well as CMCs led to energy transfer from the CDs to the surrounding polymetallic molybdates [46]. The transfer of protons from the surface of the CDs to the polymetallic molybdate molecules caused the gradually decreased fluorescence [47]. On the other hand, the polymetallic molybdates in the hydrogel absorbed the light sufficiently, resulting in the disappearance of the fluorescence behavior of the hydrogel [46]. However, the fluorescent properties of the hydrogel were restored to some extent when the ethanol solution was added dropwise to the hydrogel after photochromism. This is because ethanol, as a polar solvent, can bind competitively to polymetallic molybdate pairs of CDs. Thus, some of the carbon dots may be released from the interaction with the polymetallic molybdate and react with ethanol, resulting in some fluorescence recovery [48].

The more profound mechanism of photochromism for CCA hydrogel was explored by FTIR and XPS analysis [49]. There was a significant difference in the FTIR of CCA-0.2 hydrogels before and after UV irradiation (Fig. 5c and Fig. S11). Firstly, the overlapping peaks of OH and NH at 3600–3200 cm−1 became wider and weaker after UV irradiation. This indicated that some H+ on CMC and CDs transferred to inorganic components [24]. Then the asymmetric CH stretching band shifted from 2924 cm−1 to 2928 cm−1 after UV irradiation, increasing the fluidity of the alkyl chain. It showed that the order of the alkyl chain of hydrogel was slightly poor after UV irradiation [50]. Finally, the peaks at 920 and 798 cm−1 became wider and weaker, and the peaks at 875 cm−1 disappeared. It showed that the Mo element appeared in the form of a mixed-state structure in the hydrogel after UV irradiation [24]. Before UV irradiation, two peaks of elemental Mo were present at binding energies of 232.5 and 235.6 eV, attributed to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Mo6+, respectively (Fig. 5d-e) [51]. After 6 min of UV irradiation, the two peaks of Mo6+ became asymmetric and underwent a slight redshift. They were broken down into two groups of peaks. The characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 232.6 and 235.8 eV were attributed to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Mo6+, and the characteristic peaks at the binding energy of 231.2 and 234.8 eV were attributed to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 of Mo5+ [22,24]. The appearance of the latter indicated that the Mo element in the hydrogel existed in the mixed form of Mo6+ and Mo5+ after UV irradiation, which was consistent with the results of FTIR analysis. Therefore, photochemical reactions took place inside the hydrogel under the condition of ultraviolet light irradiation, which led to O → Mo charge transfer and partial reduction of Mo6+ to Mo5+. Mo6+ is colorless [22,23], while Mo5+ is blue. The charge transferred between Mo6+ and Mo5+ led to the color change from colorless to blue. Since the original color of the hydrogel was light yellow, the color of the hydrogel was dark green due to the mixing of blue and yellow after photochromism. After the photochromism of the hydrogel, the oxygen in the air captured an electron from Mo5+ to oxidize Mo5+ to Mo6+ [22,24]. Therefore, the color of the hydrogel reverted from dark green to the original light yellow. The photochromic mechanism of this hydrogel was consistent with the previously reported photochromic mechanism of transition metal oxides [52].

And more, the pattern printing process shows that the hydrogel has excellent photochromic characteristics and can form different patterns on surface (Fig. 5f). Concretely, the templates with different patterns were placed on the surface of the hydrogel to obtain the photochromic hydrogels with different patterns after irradiated under UV light for 6 min. Moreover, in an air environment, the surface pattern of the photochromic hydrogel gradually returned to the original color within 6 h. The results show that the CCA-0.2 hydrogel can be erased and rewritten by multiple times, and has excellent reversible repeatable patterning and information recording capabilities. The encryption/decryption of protected information can be realized by taking advantage of its characteristic of fluorescence quenching at the same time as photochromism. As shown in Fig. 5g, a mold of the word “GXU” was covered with the hydrogel and irradiated under ultraviolet light for 6 min, and after removing the mold, the hydrogel showed a green “GXU” pattern on the surface due to photochromism. Meanwhile, the fluorescent color in the UV-irradiated area appears black, while the protected area maintains the initial fluorescent color. The text pattern on the surface of the photochromic hydrogel faded naturally after 6 h at room temperature, but the black text pattern was still displayed in specific areas under UV irradiation. Importantly, by dropping the ethanol solution, the hydrogel became white, and the black “GXU” character gradually faded under the fluorescence until it disappeared, and then the fluorescence was restored. In summary, the hydrogels in this study demonstrated tunable fluorescence/photochromic switching capability and excellent patterning performance, which make them suitable for the application in reversible encryption/decryption of information. This is achieved not only through the realization of the writing and erasing of information by photochromism, but also through the realization of the hiding and displaying of encrypted information by the reversible photochromism/fluorescence switching of the hydrogels.

In this study, a kind of CCA hydrogel with promising mechanical strength, durable fluorescence response, repeatable photochromism and notable fluorescence/photochromism conversion has been successfully fabricated. This strategy enables CMC, AA, CDs and AMT to form a new complex network through free radical grafting copolymerization and embedding in the hydrothermal process. Interestingly, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of the hydrogel occurs gradually with increasing UV irradiation time, but can be selectively reversed by ethanol treatment. The reversible storage/erasure function of multiple optical information is expected to be applied to the encryption/decryption of information in pattern anti-counterfeiting. Therefore, this responsive hydrogel paves the way for the prospective applications of cellulose-based organic/inorganic hybrid cross-linked hydrogels in optical storage devices, information anti-counterfeiting and other fields.

Yu Zhong: Writing – review & editing. Zhiqi Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Lingqi Quan: Writing – review & editing. Yiqiang Wu: Conceptualization. Dongying Hu: Writing – review & editing. Jun Cheng: Visualization. Yanjie Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Fangchao Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.09.204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of transition metals on photoinduced proton transfer from anthrone to 9-anthrol in glasses prepared by the sol-gel method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Fujii, N. Tanaka, K. Kodaira, Y. Kawai, H. Yamashita, M. Anpo, Effect of transition metals on photoinduced proton transfer from anthrone to 9-anthrol in glasses prepared by the sol-gel method, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A-Chem. 125(1-3) (1999) 85-91. DOI: 10.1016/s1010-6030(99)00093-3.
